mainText:
Good morning. Here are your headlines for Sunday 24 May 2026.

AI models and products. Subquadratic's SubQ model launched May 5 with 12 million token context window using non-transformer sparse attention architecture.

UK personal finance. Trading 212 tops easy access ISAs at 4.62%, while fixed rates hit 4.72%.

Israeli politics. Coalition Parliament dissolution bill passed preliminary reading with 110 lawmakers in favour.

UK politics. Labour lost 1,100 seats while Reform UK gained 1,400 in local elections.

Sleep and wearables. Google's Fitbit Air at 99 dollars brings sleep stages and HRV to your wrist without subscription, shipping May 26.

B2B SaaS and go-to-market. Corgi raised 160 million dollars in Series B at a 1.3 billion dollar valuation.

Today's question: With AI SaaS companies trading at a 70 percent valuation premium at Series A but investors now demanding proof that AI is genuinely core to value, how are you positioning AI in your own go-to-market advisory to separate signal from the hype your clients are chasing?
fullText:
Good morning. Here is your daily brief for Sunday 24 May 2026.

🤖 AI models and products
- Gemini 3.5 Flash is now generally available with frontier-level intelligence at 4x speed and pricing at $1.50/$9 per 1M tokens with 1M context window.
- Alibaba released Qwen3 Coder Next on May 18, a code-specialist variant designed for multi-file and large-repo coding tasks with long-context support.
- Zyphra released ZAYA1-8B as an Apache 2.0 open-source MoE model with 8B total parameters trained end-to-end on AMD Instinct hardware, available on Hugging Face.

💷 UK personal finance
- Trading 212 offers the best easy-access cash ISA at 4.62% for new money, alongside fixed options from Secure Trust Bank reaching 4.72% for two-year terms, giving tax-free returns well above current inflation.
- Mortgage rates have started to fall in May 2026 after rapid hikes caused by Middle East conflict affecting swap rates, with HSBC at 4.69% for five-year remortgages and Nationwide at 4.40% for two-year purchases now available.
- The Bank of England held rates at 3.75% on 30 April and indicated likelihood of higher rates ahead, with the next MPC decision scheduled for 18 June 2026; energy price uncertainty makes cuts unlikely before autumn at earliest.

🇮🇱 Israeli politics
- Netanyahu told ultra-Orthodox political leaders he would not advance military draft exemption legislation before elections, triggering the crisis.
- Degel HaTorah, ultra-Orthodox coalition faction, said "We have no trust in Netanyahu anymore" and called for dissolving parliament.
- Naftali Bennett and Yair Lapid are seeking to broaden their opposition coalition by inviting Gadi Eisenkot, former Israeli Defence Forces chief of staff and leader of the Yashar Party.

🇬🇧 UK politics
- Keir Starmer faced calls to resign from Labour MPs including Clive Lewis, but told reporters he planned a 10-year renewal project and would lead the party into the next election.
- Nigel Farage's Reform UK took control of Havering as its first London local authority alongside Essex county council and Sunderland.
- In the London borough of Hackney, the Green Party won its first elected mayor as Zoe Garbett ousted the Labour incumbent.

😴 Sleep and wearables
- Fitbit Air announced May 7, shipping May 26, offers sleep stages, heart rate variability, blood oxygen and atrial fibrillation alerts at 99.99 dollars with no required subscription.
- Whoop with its new algorithm performed nearly as well as the Oura Ring, especially for REM and light sleep, though lagged in awake time detection.
- Oura Generation 3 and 4 demonstrated highest accuracy for resting heart rate and HRV with concordance correlations of 0.97 and 0.98, outperforming Whoop which showed moderate agreement at 0.91.

📈 B2B SaaS and go-to-market
- Corgi, an AI-native insurance platform backed by TCV, reached unicorn status four months after its 108 million dollar Series A.
- Enter, a São Paulo legal AI agent, raised 100 million dollars at a 1.2 billion dollar valuation, making it Latin America's first AI unicorn.
- AI SaaS companies command roughly 70 percent premium at Series A versus non-AI peers, though investors increasingly demand proof that AI is core to the product's value proposition.

Today's question: With AI SaaS companies trading at a 70 percent valuation premium at Series A but investors now demanding proof that AI is genuinely core to value, how are you positioning AI in your own go-to-market advisory to separate signal from the hype your clients are chasing?
